children.value:
We now have the graphs and properties of sine, cosine, and tangent firmly in
hand. The next step is to understand the algebraic relationships between them.
These relationships, known as trigonometric identities, are the tools we will
reach for whenever we need to simplify a trigonometric expression, rewrite an
integral into a manageable form, or combine two sinusoidal signals into one.
You know the relationship between sine and cosine:

children.html: cos⁡(α)=sin⁡ ⁣(α+π2)\cos(\alpha) = \sin\!\left(\alpha + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)cos(α)=sin(α+2π​)
sin⁡(α)=cos⁡ ⁣(α−π2)\sin(\alpha) = \cos\!\left(\alpha - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)sin(α)=cos(α−2π​)
sin⁡2(α)+cos⁡2(α)=1\sin^2(\alpha) + \cos^2(\alpha) = 1sin2(α)+cos2(α)=1

children.value: and can apply it to simplify expressions.
for sine and cosine and can apply them:

children.html: sin⁡(α±β)=sin⁡(α)cos⁡(β)±cos⁡(α)sin⁡(β),\sin(\alpha \pm \beta) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\beta) \pm \cos(\alpha)\sin(\beta),sin(α±β)=sin(α)cos(β)±cos(α)sin(β),
cos⁡(α±β)=cos⁡(α)cos⁡(β)∓sin⁡(α)sin⁡(β).\cos(\alpha \pm \beta) = \cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) \mp \sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta).cos(α±β)=cos(α)cos(β)∓sin(α)sin(β).

something striking: the two curves have exactly the same shape. The cosine
graph looks like the sine graph shifted to the left by

this by reading off the unit circle.

children.value:
Two immediate consequences are worth noting. First, we can express sine in
terms of cosine and vice versa:

children.html: sin⁡(α)=±1−cos⁡2(α),cos⁡(α)=±1−sin⁡2(α),\sin(\alpha) = \pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\alpha)}, \qquad \cos(\alpha) = \pm\sqrt{1 - \sin^2(\alpha)},sin(α)=±1−cos2(α)​,cos(α)=±1−sin2(α)​,

children.html: sin⁡2(α)cos⁡2(α)+1=1cos⁡2(α),\frac{\sin^2(\alpha)}{\cos^2(\alpha)} + 1 = \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha)},cos2(α)sin2(α)​+1=cos2(α)1​,
tan⁡2(α)+1=1cos⁡2(α).\tan^2(\alpha) + 1 = \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha)}.tan2(α)+1=cos2(α)1​.

children.value:
This identity appears in the computation of certain integrals and in the
analysis of vibrating systems.

children.title: Pythagorean Identities - Examples & Practice Problems, Trigonometry

children.value:
The addition theorems give the sine and cosine of a sum or difference of two
angles in terms of the sines and cosines of the individual angles:

children.html: sin⁡(α+β)=sin⁡(α)cos⁡(β)+cos⁡(α)sin⁡(β),\sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\beta) + \cos(\alpha)\sin(\beta),sin(α+β)=sin(α)cos(β)+cos(α)sin(β),
sin⁡(α−β)=sin⁡(α)cos⁡(β)−cos⁡(α)sin⁡(β),\sin(\alpha - \beta) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\beta) - \cos(\alpha)\sin(\beta),sin(α−β)=sin(α)cos(β)−cos(α)sin(β),
cos⁡(α+β)=cos⁡(α)cos⁡(β)−sin⁡(α)sin⁡(β),\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) - \sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta),cos(α+β)=cos(α)cos(β)−sin(α)sin(β),
cos⁡(α−β)=cos⁡(α)cos⁡(β)+sin⁡(α)sin⁡(β).\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) + \sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta).cos(α−β)=cos(α)cos(β)+sin(α)sin(β).

children.value: These four formulas can be written more compactly using the

children.html: ∓\mp∓

children.value:
notation, as in the learning goals, but we write them out in full here for
clarity.
We use the addition theorem to compute an exact value of

children.html: sin⁡(75°)=sin⁡(5π/12)\sin(75°) = \sin(5\pi/12)sin(75°)=sin(5π/12)
75°=45°+30°75° = 45° + 30°75°=45°+30°
sin⁡(75°)=sin⁡(45°+30°)=sin⁡(45°)cos⁡(30°)+cos⁡(45°)sin⁡(30°).\sin(75°) = \sin(45° + 30°) = \sin(45°)\cos(30°) + \cos(45°)\sin(30°).sin(75°)=sin(45°+30°)=sin(45°)cos(30°)+cos(45°)sin(30°).

children.value: Substituting the standard values from the previous section:

children.value:
Suppose two sinusoidal signals of the same
frequency but different phase shifts are added together:

children.html: f(t)=A1sin⁡(ωt+φ1)+A2sin⁡(ωt+φ2).f(t) = A_1 \sin(\omega t + \varphi_1) + A_2 \sin(\omega t + \varphi_2).f(t)=A1​sin(ωt+φ1​)+A2​sin(ωt+φ2​).

children.value:
Expanding each term using the addition theorem for sine turns this into a sum
of terms involving

children.html: sin⁡(ωt)\sin(\omega t)sin(ωt)
cos⁡(ωt)\cos(\omega t)cos(ωt)

children.value:
, which can then be
combined into a single sinusoid. This technique, expanding and recombining
trigonometric expressions, is fundamental in the analysis of vibrations and
electrical circuits.
Video “Sum and Difference Identities” by The Organic Chemistry Tutor

children.title:
Sum and Difference Identities & Formulas - Sine, Cosine, Tangent - Degrees &
Radians, Trigonometry

children.html: sin⁡(2α)=sin⁡(α+α)=sin⁡(α)cos⁡(α)+cos⁡(α)sin⁡(α)=2sin⁡(α)cos⁡(α).\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(\alpha + \alpha) = \sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha) + \cos(\alpha)\sin(\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha).sin(2α)=sin(α+α)=sin(α)cos(α)+cos(α)sin(α)=2sin(α)cos(α).

children.value: For cosine there are three equivalent forms, all obtained from

children.html: cos⁡(2α)=cos⁡2(α)−sin⁡2(α)\cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha)cos(2α)=cos2(α)−sin2(α)
cos⁡(2α)=cos⁡2(α)−sin⁡2(α)=2cos⁡2(α)−1=1−2sin⁡2(α).\cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha) = 2\cos^2(\alpha) - 1 = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha).cos(2α)=cos2(α)−sin2(α)=2cos2(α)−1=1−2sin2(α).

children.value:
The three forms of the cosine double angle formula are useful in different
contexts. Rearranging the second and third forms gives the

children.html: cos⁡2(α)=1+cos⁡(2α)2,sin⁡2(α)=1−cos⁡(2α)2.\cos^2(\alpha) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\alpha)}{2}, \qquad \sin^2(\alpha) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\alpha)}{2}.cos2(α)=21+cos(2α)​,sin2(α)=21−cos(2α)​.

children.value:
These formulas replace a squared trigonometric function with an unsquared one
at double the angle. They appear constantly in integral calculus: computing
the integral of

children.html: sin⁡2(α)\sin^2(\alpha)sin2(α)
cos⁡2(α)\cos^2(\alpha)cos2(α)

children.value:
directly is awkward, but
after applying the power reduction formula the integral becomes straightforward.
We will use them in exactly this way when we reach integration.

children.title: Double Angle Identities & Formulas of Sin, Cos & Tan - Trigonometry

children.value: Video “Trig identities” by The Organic Chemistry Tutor

The three groups of identities in this section form the core algebraic toolkit
for trigonometric calculations. The sine-cosine phase relationship shows that
the two functions are the same oscillation viewed from a different starting
point. The Pythagorean identity connects the two functions through the geometry
of the unit circle and generates further useful identities by simple algebra.
The addition theorems allow any sum or difference of angles to be expanded, and
the double angle formulas follow as special cases. With these identities, and
the power reduction formulas in particular, we are fully prepared for the
integration techniques we will encounter in a later chapter. In the next section we introduce the concept of limits, which forms the
bridge from functions to differential calculus. For those interested, section
4.7 gives an optional introduction to the hyperbolic functions, which share a
striking structural similarity with the trigonometric functions but are built
from the exponential function.
